1: Wayyosep ’iyyov se’et mishlo (Job again continued his discourse) — the same formula as 27:1, confirming that chs 27–31 form a single extended mashal unit. Chapter 29 begins Job’s nostalgic lament for the days before his suffering. The speech covers three movements: the former blessing (ch29), the present suffering (ch30), and the oath of innocence (ch31). Chapter 29 is the OT’s most extended first-person portrait of righteous social leadership.
2: Mi-yiteneni kiyarhe-qedem (oh that I were as in the months of old) — mi-yiteneni = “who will give to me” = the formula of longing/wish (“oh that...!”). Yarhe-qedem = months of qedem (the past, antiquity, the east direction associated with origins). Kimei ’elohah yishmereni = “in the days when God watched over me” — shamar = to keep, guard, watch over (the verb of the Aaronic blessing: YHWH “keep you,” Num 6:24). Job remembers divine watchfulness as blessing; now he experiences it (in his suffering) as curse.
3: Behillo nero ’alei ro’shi (when his lamp shone over my head) — nerot = lamp; hilo = its shining (infinitive of halal = to shine/praise). The lamp over the head is the divine light of favor and guidance (Ps 18:28: “you light my lamp”; Ps 119:105: “your word is a lamp to my feet”). Ve’or-lo elek-hoshekh = “and by his light I walked through darkness” — the darkness (hoshekh) was navigated by divine light. This verse is the inversion of Job’s current experience: now he walks in darkness with no lamp.
4: Ka’asher hayiti bimei horpi (as I was in the days of my autumn/prime) — horef = autumn/harvest season (the most productive season of the agricultural year). Job’s prime of life is described as his “autumn” — the season of maximum fruit, maturity, and harvest. Besod ’eloah ’ali ’ahali = “when the friendship/counsel of God was over my tent” — sod = intimate circle, secret counsel, confidential gathering (Ps 25:14: “the sod of YHWH is for those who fear him”; Jer 23:18: “who has stood in the sod of YHWH?”). Job remembers being in YHWH’s inner circle of counsel — the most intimate form of divine relationship.
5: Besod ’adonay yesaret shaddai ’imadi (when the Almighty was still with me) — ’imadi = with me (the divine presence with the person). Vesevivotay ne’aray = “when my children were around me” — the children who were killed in ch1 are remembered as surrounding Job in his former prosperity. The contrast is acute: the children who circled Job in happiness now lie dead.
6: Birhots halikkay behemath (when I bathed my steps in cream/curds) — rahas = to wash/bathe; hemath = curds/cream/butter (the product of cows, here used for extreme agricultural abundance). Vetsur yatsaq ’imadi palgei-shamen = “and the rock poured out for me streams of olive oil” — palgei shamen = streams of oil. The image of oil flowing from solid rock echoes Deut 32:13 (yinqehu shemen mehalhammish tsur = “they sucked oil from the flinty rock”). The abundance is so extreme that rock itself pours oil — the most infertile substrate becomes a source of the most precious agricultural product.
7: Betseti sha’ar ’alei-qaret (when I went out to the gate over the city) — sha’ar = the city gate, the site of judicial proceedings, commercial transactions, and social meeting in the ancient Israelite/Near Eastern city (Deut 22:15; Ruth 4:1; Amos 5:10). Barehov ’akhinu moshavi = “when I prepared/set my seat in the public square” — rehhov = the broad open square near the gate; mokhehin = I set/prepared (from kun). Job took his judicial seat in the city’s public space — the position of the judge and leader.
8: Ra’uni ne’arim venikhba’u (the young men saw me and stepped aside/hid themselves) — nikba’u = hid themselves, stepped back (niphal of khaba’ = to hide). The young men’s retreat was a gesture of deference, not fear. Veqenim qamu ’amadu = “and the aged rose and stood to their feet” — the elders (qenim = aged men) rising in respect is a gesture of honor that the ancient world required for leaders and sages (Lev 19:32: “rise before the gray head”).
9: Sarim ’atseru bimillim (princes held back their words) — sarim = princes/officials; ’atsaru = restrained, held back (from ’atsar = to restrain). The highest social class — the princes — fell silent before Job’s wisdom. Vekhaf yasimmu lefiyhem = “and put their hand over their mouth” — the gesture of respectful silence in the presence of greater wisdom (Job 40:4 where Job himself will make this gesture before YHWH from the whirlwind).
10: Qol negidim nistatar (the voice of the nobles was hidden/fell silent) — nagid = a leader, ruler, commander (used of David as the designated king before his coronation, 1 Sam 25:30). Uleshon lehekhham davaqah = “and their tongue stuck to the roof of their mouths” — davaq = to cling, stick to (the same verb as Ruth 1:14, “Ruth clung to her mother-in-law”). Complete, involuntary silence before Job’s presence.
11: Ki ’ozen sheme’ah vateashereni (for the ear that heard me called me blessed) — ’ishar = to pronounce blessed/happy (the same root as the ’ashrei beatitudes of Ps 1:1; Matt 5:3–11). Ve’ayin ra’atah vate’ideli = “and the eye that saw me testified for me” — both the hearing and seeing of Job’s conduct brought forth spontaneous testimony to his righteousness. The two-witness principle of Deut 19:15 (al-pi shnayim ’edim) is invoked as Job’s universal community endorsement.
12: Ki ’amallet ’ani meshavea’ (for I rescued the poor when they cried out) — amallet = I rescued/delivered (piel of palat = to escape, deliver). ’ani meshavea’ = the poor one crying out (shavea’ from shavac = to cry for help; cf. meshavea’ in the Psalms as the cry of the oppressed). Veyatom velo-’ozer lo = “and the fatherless who had no helper” — yatom = orphan (one of the three protected groups in covenant law alongside the widow and stranger, Exod 22:21–22).
13: Birkat ’oved ’alai tavo’ (the blessing of the one perishing came upon me) — ’oved = the one perishing/dying (participle of ’abad = to perish). The blessing of the dying person — the final words of a dying man — is the most precious form of blessing in the ancient world (cf. Jacob’s deathbed blessing, Gen 49). Job received these from those he rescued. Velev ’almanah ’arnin = “and the widow’s heart I caused to sing/ring for joy” — ’arnin (hiphil of ranan = to ring out in joy). The widow is the second of the three protected covenant classes.
14: Tsedaqah lavashti vatilbasheni (I put on righteousness and it clothed me) — the Hebrew is reflexive: Job clothed himself in tsedaqah (righteousness), and it clothed him in return. The reflexive structure suggests that righteousness is not merely worn like a garment but becomes internalized — the garment takes possession of the wearer. Kemeil umitsaneph mishpati = “my justice was like a robe and turban” — me’il = the outer robe (worn by Samuel, 1 Sam 2:19; by the high priest, Lev 8:7; by princes); tsanif = turban/headdress (worn by the high priest, Exod 28:4). The two vestments are priestly and royal — Job’s justice clothed him in the robes of the double office.
15: ’Einayim hayiti la’iver (I was eyes for the blind) — ’einayim = eyes. Job’s justice function was literally to see on behalf of those who could not see (the blind) — to represent their interests in legal proceedings. Veraglaim la’isser ’ani = “and feet for the lame” — ’isser = lame (from ’isar = to be hobbled/lame). The double disability of blindness and lameness appears together in 2 Sam 5:6–8 (the Jebusites taunting David) and in Isa 35:5–6 (the messianic restoration). Job personifies the remedying of these disabilities.
16: ’Av ’anokhi la’evyonim (I was a father to the needy) — ’av = father; the patriarchal-protector role taken on behalf of those without a natural advocate. Veriv lo-yadati ’ehqerehu = “and I investigated the case of the one I did not know” — riv = a legal dispute/lawsuit (the same word as the covenant lawsuit form); haqar = to probe/investigate thoroughly. Job not only helped those he knew; he took on the cases of complete strangers, investigating their legal claims.
17: Va’ashabbera metsallot ’avval (I broke the fangs/jaws of the wicked) — metsallot = jaws, fangs (of a predatory animal); the wicked is a predator with fangs. Shabbera = I broke/shattered (piel). Umishinav ’ashlikh tered = “and from his teeth I forced the prey” — tered = prey (what is torn); Job forcibly extracted the victim from the wicked’s grip. The metaphor: Job as the anti-predator, rescuing prey from the jaws of the powerful wicked.
18: Va’omar ’im-qinni ’egva’ (then I said “in my nest I shall die”) — qen = nest; dying in one’s nest = dying at home surrounded by family and comfort, like a bird dying in its nest. The image is of a peaceful, natural, dignified death at the end of a long, productive life. Vekhahol ’arbeh yamim = “and I shall multiply my days like sand” — khahol = sand (the same comparison used for Abraham’s promised offspring, Gen 22:17). The expectation of long life, many descendants, peaceful death.
19: Shorshi patua’ ’el-mayim (my roots spread out to the water) — the tree-by-water imagery for righteous prosperity (Ps 1:3; Jer 17:8). Vetal yalin biqtsiri = “and the dew rested all night on my branches” — tal = dew (the nightly moisture that kept plants alive in the dry season; a symbol of divine blessing, Gen 27:28; Deut 33:28). Job describes his former life as a well-watered tree, rooted deep and refreshed nightly.
20: Kevodi hadash ’imadi (my glory was fresh with me) — kavod = glory/honor/weight (the same word used for YHWH’s glory in theophanies). Job’s honor was perpetually fresh, not diminishing. Veqashti beyadi tahalif = “and my bow was continually renewed in my hand” — the bow as a symbol of military and social power (Gen 49:24, Joseph’s bow remains firm; 1 Sam 18:4, Jonathan’s bow given to David). A bow “renewed” is a bow kept at full tension, ready for use.
21: Li shomeu veyehhelu (men listened to me and waited) — shomeu = they listened (with focused attention, the kind of hearing that leads to action). Vedammu lemodati = “and held their silence for my counsel” — damam = to be silent, still (cf. Ps 46:10: “be still and know that I am God”). The community’s posture before Job: attentive silence, waiting for his wisdom.
22: ’Ahar davari lo yishnnu (after my words they spoke no more) — Job’s words were so comprehensive and authoritative that follow-up discussion was unnecessary. Vetipah ’alay millatam = “and my speech fell upon them” — tipah = to drip/fall (as rain). Job’s word falling on people like rain echoes the anticipation of v23 (they waited for him as for rain).
23: Veyihhalu ki-malqosh (they waited for me as for the latter rain) — malqosh = the spring/latter rain (March-April in Israel, the second rainfall season; the critical late rain that filled the grain). The former rain (yoreh) opens the planting season; the malqosh completes the grain. Job’s words were as critically awaited as the spring rain — without which the entire season’s crop would fail. Ufehem patsou limlaqosh = “and they opened their mouths wide as for the spring rain” — the gesture of opening the mouth wide to receive falling rain from heaven.
24: ’Eshoq ’alehem lo ya’aminu (when I smiled they could hardly believe it) — shoq = to smile, laugh (used of YHWH laughing/mocking in Ps 2:4 and 37:13). Job’s smile of approval was so valued that receiving it was almost unbelievable to the recipient. Ve’or panay lo yappilu = “and the light of my face they did not let fall” — the “light of the face” is the approval and favor radiating from a leader’s expression (Num 6:25: “YHWH make his face shine upon you”). Job’s community treasured his approval as they treasured divine favor.
25: ’Evchar darkam ve’eshev ro’sh (I chose the course for them and sat as head/chief) — ro’sh = head, chief (the primary leader). Job’s leadership was not just reactive but directive: he chose (bahar) their path. Va’eshkon kemayim begedud = “I dwelt as a king among his troops” — gedud = a troop/raiding band; the military unit whose king lives among them. Job was not an absent leader but one who lived within his community. Ka’asher ’avelim yenahhem = “as one who comforts mourners” — naham = to comfort (the same verb used of the friends’ mission at 2:11, lenahhamo). The final verse of Job’s nostalgic portrait identifies the supreme mark of his former leadership: he comforted those who mourned.
